common_name: White Icicle Radish (Long White Radish, Lady Finger Radish)

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked, or in fall 4–6 weeks before first frost; White Icicle is an elongated, all-white heirloom radish producing 4–6 inch cylindrical roots that remain crisp, mild, and tender at larger sizes than typical round red varieties — making it a preferred choice for fresh eating and cooking when the texture and flavor of a larger, milder radish are desired.

directions: Sow directly 1/4 inch deep and thin to 2–3 inches apart; White Icicle is faster-maturing than daikon (25–35 days vs. 60+ days) and milder than Chinese black radish, fitting into the cool-season rotation between spring planting and summer heat; harvest before roots reach maximum size for finest quality. White Icicle is a classic heirloom radish that has been grown in American gardens since the 19th century and appears in Victorian-era seed catalogs as one of the standard garden radish varieties. Unlike the small, round, red radishes (Cherry Belle, Champion, Sparkler) that dominate modern radish offerings, White Icicle produces elongated, cylindrical, all-white roots 4–6 inches long with a gentle taper — resembling a large, smooth, pale daikon in miniature. The flavor is milder and less pungent than round red varieties at the same stage of maturity, and the texture is firm and crisp without the slight woodiness that can develop in large round varieties. White Icicle is excellent for fresh eating in salads (the white color is elegant against greens), for cutting into sticks for crudités platters, for Japanese-style pickles, for cooking in stir-fries (where it holds its shape better than rounder types), and for the traditional French preparation of radishes served with butter and salt. The elongated shape also makes White Icicle more practical for slicing in certain applications where round radishes are inconvenient.

common_name: Misato Rose Radish (Beauty Heart Radish, Rose Heart Radish)

planting.note: Best as a fall crop — direct sow in late summer 10–6 weeks before first frost; Misato Rose is a Japanese winter radish with green or greenish-white exterior skin and a spectacular vivid rose to magenta-pink interior flesh — one of the most visually stunning root vegetables available, making it a centerpiece of any vegetable display and exceptionally attractive in slaws, salads, and pickles where the color impact is full.

directions: Direct sow in late summer or fall; thin to 4–6 inches for full root development; Misato Rose grows to 3–4 inches in diameter over 60–70 days — much longer than spring radishes; harvest before hard freeze; the vivid interior color intensifies in cool fall weather; slice thin to showcase the color in salads. Misato Rose is a traditional Japanese winter radish (daikon type) from the Misato region of Japan, where it has been grown for centuries as a specialty vegetable known for its extraordinary interior color. The exterior of the root is pale green to greenish-white — completely unremarkable from the outside — but when the root is cut crosswise, the interior reveals a brilliant ring of vivid rose to magenta-pink extending from the interior outward, with a paler white core and a narrow white zone just inside the green skin. The effect is striking: the pink-fleshed cross-section looks like a small decorative disc with a rose-colored center zone. The vivid pink color comes from betalain pigments similar to those in beets and watermelon radishes, and it intensifies dramatically in the cooler temperatures of fall growing. The flavor is mild, slightly sweet, and crisp — much milder than the small red spring radishes most American gardeners know, and excellent for slicing thin in Japanese-style pickles (where the pink color turns the pickling liquid a beautiful deep rose), for raw salads where the color provides visual drama, and for Korean-style seasoned vegetable preparations. Also called 'Beauty Heart Radish' in some markets.

common_name: Jenny Lind Melon (Early American Heirloom Ribbed Muskmelon)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 70°F; Jenny Lind is a small (1–2 lb), very heavily ribbed, deeply lobed heirloom muskmelon with distinctive turban-like bumps at the blossom end and unusually light green to apple-green flesh — one of the oldest American named melon varieties (dating to the 1840s), named for the famous Swedish opera singer Jenny Lind who toured America in 1851 to enormous popular acclaim.

directions: Direct sow in hills after last frost; Jenny Lind melons are small and compact compared to modern varieties, making them suitable for small gardens; harvest when the melon slips easily from the vine with gentle pressure and the blossom-end softens slightly; the green flesh is unusual for American melons and has a delicate, sweet, slightly floral flavor. Jenny Lind is one of the oldest and most historically documented named melon varieties in American horticulture — it was listed in seed catalogs by the 1840s and became extremely popular in the northeastern states (particularly New Jersey, where it was widely grown for the Philadelphia and New York markets) during the latter half of the 19th century, remaining a garden standard well into the early 20th century before modern hybrids displaced it. The variety is named in honor of Jenny Lind (1820–1887), the Swedish opera singer known as the 'Swedish Nightingale,' whose American tour managed by P.T. Barnum in 1850–1851 created an extraordinary popular sensation — everything from hairstyles to trains to songs to garden plants were named for her in that era. Jenny Lind melon is distinctive in several ways: the small (typically 1–2 pound) round fruits are deeply ribbed with prominent ridges running from stem to blossom end, and the blossom end has a characteristic raised, button-like knob in the center of a depression — a 'turban' or 'navel' that is unique among common muskmelons. The flesh is a pale sea-green to apple-green color (not the familiar salmon-orange of modern cantaloupes) that is thin relative to the total fruit size but very sweet and floral in flavor. Jenny Lind ripens about 2 weeks earlier than standard cantaloupes, making it an excellent choice for short-season gardens.
sources: Fearing Burr Jr. Field and Garden Vegetables of America (1863)

planting.note: Direct sow as early as soil can be worked in spring or 4–6 weeks before first fall frost; Sparkler is a fast-maturing spring radish with a distinctive two-toned coloring — vivid red on the upper two-thirds of the root and bright white on the lower third — that makes it one of the most visually attractive spring radishes for fresh eating, relish trays, and kitchen display.

directions: Sow directly 1/4 inch deep as early as soil can be worked; thin to 2 inches apart; Sparkler matures very quickly (22–30 days) and becomes pithy and hollow if left too long in the ground — harvest promptly when roots reach 3/4 to 1 inch in diameter; succession-plant every 2–3 weeks for continuous supply. Sparkler is a classic American heirloom spring radish that dates to the late 19th century and remains a popular choice in home gardens for its distinctive bicolor appearance: the round, smooth roots are vivid scarlet-red on the upper portion and a clean, bright white on the lower third to half — a sharp, clean color transition that gives the radish a visually appealing two-toned look unlike the solid red of Cherry Belle or the pure white of White Icicle. The name 'Sparkler' is apt — the red-and-white coloring is eye-catching and festive in a salad bowl or on a relish tray. The flavor is crisp and mildly peppery, slightly milder than some all-red varieties, and the texture at perfect maturity is ideal — firm, juicy, and snap-crisp. Like all spring radishes, Sparkler bolts rapidly in summer heat and should be grown only in cool weather (spring and fall); in zone 9a, the fall season (September through November) and very early spring (January through March in coastal areas) are the practical growing windows. Sparkler is valued by gardeners who want visual variety in their radish plantings beyond the standard all-red types.

common_name: Early Scarlet Globe Radish (Classic Fast Spring Radish)

planting.note: Direct sow as early as soil is workable in spring; Early Scarlet Globe is one of the oldest and most widely grown spring radish varieties in American gardens — a very fast-maturing (22–28 days), perfectly round, bright scarlet-red radish with crisp white flesh, mild peppery flavor, and a slow tendency to become pithy after maturity, making it one of the most reliable and forgiving of the traditional spring radish varieties.

directions: Sow in thin rows 1/4 inch deep; no need to thin closely if sown sparsely; harvest when roots are 3/4 to 1 inch across; Early Scarlet Globe holds slightly better in the ground than some varieties without becoming immediately pithy — useful for succession plantings where exact harvest timing is impractical. Early Scarlet Globe is a foundational heirloom spring radish that has appeared continuously in American seed catalogs since the 19th century and remains one of the most widely recognized and consistently reliable spring radish varieties for home garden use. The name describes it precisely: the variety matures early, produces globe-shaped (round, not oval or elongated) roots, and has the classic bright scarlet skin color of traditional spring radishes. The roots are smooth and round, typically reaching 3/4 to 1 inch in diameter at the ideal harvest stage, with bright white, crisp, mildly peppery flesh that is refreshing and clean-flavored. Early Scarlet Globe is somewhat more forgiving than some spring radish varieties — it maintains quality for a few extra days in the ground after reaching harvest size before becoming pithy or hollow — a useful characteristic when garden timing is imprecise. Its very fast maturity (22–28 days) makes it one of the quickest-maturing vegetables a gardener can grow and ideal for teaching children about gardening: almost immediate gratification. In zone 9a and the Gulf South, Early Scarlet Globe thrives in the short cool season windows of January–March and October–November before heat forces bolting.
